Quick Bio

MS Financial Engineering from the University of Southern California (GPA 3.84). Graduate Researcher at USC. Previously Quantitative Research Intern at New England Investment Consulting Group.

I work on quantitative research and systematic trading strategies, combining machine learning, stochastic processes, and market microstructure insights to develop scalable investment models. My work focuses on building end-to-end quantitative pipelines — data engineering, model calibration, backtesting, and portfolio optimization for derivatives and systematic portfolios.

I have previously worked across quantitative research, financial management, and policy analysis, bringing both strong technical modeling expertise and practical experience in real-world investment decision-making. My interests lie in quantitative research, systematic trading, derivatives strategy, and portfolio analytics.

Andromeda

Proprietary Options Alpha Detection Platform

Production trading system scanning live options chains across multiple tickers. Surfaces highest-conviction mispriced contracts with full portfolio analytics, Greeks attribution, and risk management.

Landing & Configuration
Multi-ticker selection with DTE range sliders, risk-free rate controls, and investment sizing. Four-module architecture: Volatility Engine, Smart Screening, Portfolio Analytics, and Run History.
Portfolio Dashboard
Real-time portfolio view showing spot prices, contract count, average edge, IV edge, and 24h intraday price charts for TSLA, NVDA, and SPY. Includes premium summary, Sharpe ratio (2.788), win rate (92.1%), and capital deployment metrics.
Greeks Analysis
Per-contract Greeks table with delta, gamma, theta, vega, market IV, and win rate. Interactive charts showing |Delta|, Gamma, Theta decay, and Vega sensitivity curves across strike prices.
P&L Scenarios
Best/expected/worst-case P&L scenarios at expiry. Per-contract scenario bar chart with grouped best (green), expected (orange), and worst (red) outcomes. Detailed scenario table with fair value, market price, and net premium.
Hedge Contracts
Protective leg construction — for each short position, a further-OTM option is purchased as protection. Net credit maintained on every spread with full hedge cost and max loss tracking.

Python · Streamlit · Proprietary Volatility Engine · SQLite · Polygon.io

Two published arXiv preprints spanning NLP-driven equity signals and computational option pricing frameworks. Research validated out-of-sample with real-world profitability, targeting top finance journals.

Options portfolio construction and stochastic volatility modeling

Beyond the numbers

The person behind the quantitative models

When I'm not calibrating volatility surfaces or training FinBERT models, you'll find me exploring Los Angeles, building intricate Lego sets, or chasing the perfect photograph. I moved to LA for USC's MS Financial Engineering program and fell in love with the city's energy — from the Pacific sunsets to the late-night taco trucks.

I believe the best quantitative thinkers are curious about everything, not just markets. Photography taught me to see patterns in light the way I see patterns in data. Lego taught me that complex systems are built one brick at a time — much like building a trading system from scratch.

Life in Los Angeles

City of Angels

The City of Angels — from driving the Pacific Coast Highway with the windows down, to catching golden-hour sunsets at the beach and hiking the Hollywood Hills. Strolling through Downtown and Beverly Hills on weekends, catching a Trojans football game at the iconic LA Coliseum — home to the Olympics 2028. Visiting Warner Bros and Universal to relive every childhood dream. What started as a move for USC became something much bigger — this place truly became my home.

Game day at the Coliseum — Olympics 2028 venue

Photography

Passion

There is something about sunsets in Los Angeles that words can never fully capture.

Every sunset is unique and tells a different story. Sometimes the sky is stormy and brooding, heavy clouds rolling in from the Pacific. Sometimes it's a clear sky with sun rays blazing through in golden columns of light. And sometimes — the magic times — the clouds catch the dying sunlight and reflect it back in the most vibrant, almost impossible colors: deep purples bleeding into coral, amber melting into rose.

This is what made me an amateur photographer. Not a desire to be one, but an inability to let these moments pass uncaptured. I have over a hundred photographs of different sunsets, each one unrepeatable. But doesn't a collection like that call for its own portfolio?

For now, here are a few favorites.

Lego Building

Creative

There's a meditative quality to snapping bricks into place at midnight that recharges the same brain that spent the day debugging volatility calibration code. Complex Lego sets are like building trading systems — you follow the instructions until you understand the architecture, then you start improvising. From a Milky Way galaxy mosaic with a tiny "You are here" marker, to a Spider-Man helmet that took an entire weekend — every build is a reminder that extraordinary things are assembled one small piece at a time.

Published Research arXiv:2604.13260

Which Voices Move Markets? Speaker Identity and the Cross-Section of Post-Earnings Returns

Authors: Karmanpartap Singh Sidhu, Junyi Fan, Maryam Pishgar
Affiliation: Viterbi School of Engineering, University of Southern California
Published: arXiv preprint, arXiv:2604.13260, April 2026
16,428
Earnings Calls
6.5M
Sentences Scored
0.142
OOS Spearman IC
2.03%
Monthly FF5 Alpha
Situation
Earnings call textual analysis treats all speakers as equal

Corporate earnings conference calls are the single most information-dense recurring disclosure in U.S. equity markets, featuring real-time unscripted interactions between management and sell-side analysts. Despite this, existing textual analysis literature treats each transcript as a monolithic document, computing aggregate sentiment scores that weight all speakers equally. A bullish statement by the CEO during scripted prepared remarks carries fundamentally different informational content than a pointed question from an analyst during Q&A. The CEO's optimism may be strategically managed; the analyst's skepticism reflects the real-time assessment of a sophisticated market participant.

Task
Decompose sentiment by speaker role and prove it predicts returns

Apply FinBERT to 6.5 million sentences from 16,428 S&P 500 quarterly earnings call transcripts (2015–2025), classify each sentence by speaker role (Analyst, CFO, Executive, Other), derive empirical weights from each speaker's predictive power, and validate through Fama-French five-factor regressions, Fama-MacBeth cross-sectional tests, earnings surprise controls, and a horse-race against Loughran-McDonald dictionaries.

Action
Five aggregation methods with IC-derived section weighting

Each transcript is parsed into speaker-attributed sentences, scored with FinBERT (net sentiment = P(positive) - P(negative)), then aggregated using five methods: Simple Mean (M1), Confidence-Weighted (M2), Extreme Fractions (M3), Section-Weighted (M4, our key contribution), and Analyst-Only (M5). Section weights are derived from training-period Spearman ICs and frozen for out-of-sample testing.

Step 01

Transcript Parsing

16,428 calls parsed, 6.5M sentences attributed to 4 speaker roles

Step 02

FinBERT Scoring

GPU-accelerated HPC, sentence-level P(pos), P(neg), P(neutral)

Step 03

IC Weight Derivation

Spearman IC per speaker category on training sample (pre-2023)

Step 04

Section-Weighted Signal

Analyst 48.8%, CFO 29.5%, Executive 15.9%, Other 5.8%

Step 05

Asset Pricing Tests

FF5 regressions, Fama-MacBeth, double-sorts, horse-race vs LM

Results
24.3% annualized alpha, signal strengthens out-of-sample
Table V: IC-Derived Section Weights
IC Weights
Analyst — 48.8% (IC = 0.128***)
CFO — 29.5% (IC = 0.078***)
Executive — 15.9% (IC = 0.042***)
Other — 5.8% (IC = 0.015)

Analysts comprise only 20.2% of sentences but carry 48.8% of predictive weight. Weights frozen from training period (pre-Jan 2023).

Table VI: Information Coefficient Comparison Across Methods
Method IC (1-day) tNW IC (5-day)
Simple Mean (M1)0.08135.49***0.0673
Confidence-Weighted (M2)0.09866.36***0.0804
Extreme Fractions (M3)0.07495.36***0.0620
Section-Weighted (M4)0.11889.11***0.0988
Analyst-Only (M5)0.140511.71***0.1171

Section-weighted (M4) yields 46% improvement over simple mean baseline. Analyst-only (M5) highest IC at 0.141.

Table VII: Out-of-Sample Validation (Signal Strengthens OOS)
Method Train IC Test IC Change
Simple Mean (M1)0.08060.0966+19.9%
Section-Weighted (M4)0.11410.1442+26.4%
Analyst-Only (M5)0.12730.1707+34.1%

All methods strengthen out-of-sample. Section-weighted IC rises from 0.114 to 0.144. This rules out overfitting.

Table XIV: Fama-French Five-Factor Alpha
Section-Wtd (M4) Analyst-Only (M5)
Monthly Alpha2.026%***2.542%***
Annualized Alpha24.31%30.51%
t-stat (Newey-West)6.498.69
R²0.0790.034

Low R² confirms the signal is largely orthogonal to known risk factors. OOS alpha rises to 2.77%/month (t = 8.56).

SUE Tercile Q1 (%) Q5 (%) Q5 − Q1 t-stat
Low SUE−2.767−0.5392.227***7.27

Sentiment is most informative when earnings disappoint (Low SUE spread = 2.23%). Positive tone during a bad quarter signals management confidence the market underweights.

Table XXI: XGBoost-SHAP Validation of Speaker Importance
Speaker SHAP % IC Weight %
Analyst59.6%48.8%
CFO29.1%29.5%
Executive8.5%15.9%
Other2.8%5.8%

XGBoost in-sample IC: 0.172, out-of-sample IC: 0.171. Near-zero decay confirms speaker hierarchy is a stable structural feature, not overfitting.

Key conclusion: FinBERT completely subsumes the Loughran-McDonald dictionary. The IC-derived section weights accurately capture structural aspects of the earnings call information environment, with signal strengthening out-of-sample (OOS alpha 2.77%/month, t = 8.56 vs training 1.56%/month).

Quantum Computing D-Wave Hardware

Quantum Annealing Correction for Credit Card Fraud Detection

90%
Peak NQAC Accuracy
20
Bayesian Iterations
QUBO
Optimization Model
NQAC
Error Correction
Situation
Feature selection for fraud detection is combinatorially explosive; quantum hardware is noisy

Statistical and machine-learning models use sets of input variables to predict fraud. Selecting from a large set of potential features a highly informative subset simplifies the model and reduces dimensionality — but the selection problem grows exponentially with feature count, making it a natural fit for quantum annealing on D-Wave hardware.

However, D-Wave quantum annealers suffer from thermal fluctuations and control errors that degrade solution quality as problem size increases. Existing quantum annealing correction (QAC) methods have limitations in generalizability and fixed code distance. A more flexible, scalable error correction scheme is needed.

Task
Design a hybrid quantum-classical pipeline that selects optimal features while correcting hardware noise

The goal is to formulate feature selection as a QUBO problem solvable on D-Wave, apply Nested Quantum Annealing Correction (NQAC) to improve solution quality, and use Bayesian Optimization to tune the error correction parameters — then validate the selected features through a downstream RandomForestClassifier on the Kaggle Credit Card Fraud Detection dataset.

Action
MIQUBO formulation + NQAC repetition code + Bayesian parameter tuning

Each dataset feature is represented as a binary variable in a QUBO model. Mutual Information (MI) between each feature and the fraud target is computed using Shannon entropy — features with high MI reduce uncertainty about the target. Linear terms in the QUBO are set to the negative MI for each feature, incentivizing selection of informative features. Quadratic terms capture conditional MI between feature pairs to handle redundancy.

Nested Quantum Annealing Correction (NQAC) then encodes each logical qubit into a complete graph of C physical qubits with ferromagnetic coupling. This belongs to the repetition code family: physical qubits are coupled to align in the same state, and majority voting decodes the logical qubit even when individual qubits flip. SpinReversalTransformComposite applies gauge transformations to average out systematic hardware biases before the nested BQM is embedded onto D-Wave via minor embedding.

Bayesian Optimization explores the parameter space of nesting_degree (3–13) and coupling_penalty (-1.0 to 1.0) over 20 iterations, with the objective function being classification accuracy on the selected feature subset.

Step 01

MI Computation

Calculate Shannon entropy and mutual information for each feature vs fraud target

Step 02

QUBO Build

Linear terms = -MI; quadratic terms = conditional MI for feature pairs; add penalty

Step 03

NQAC Nesting

Replace each logical qubit with complete graph of degree C; ferromagnetic coupling

Step 04

Gauge + Embed

SpinReversalTransform, then minor embedding onto D-Wave QPU

Step 05

Decode + Vote

Majority voting decodes logical qubits from physical qubit samples

Step 06

Bayesian Opt

20 iterations tuning nesting_degree and coupling_penalty for max accuracy

Results
NQAC outperforms uncorrected annealing — gap widens with problem complexity

Across all feature count configurations (4 to 20 features), the NQAC-corrected model achieves higher RandomForestClassifier accuracy than the non-NQAC baseline. The performance gap widens as the number of features increases — precisely when hardware noise becomes more impactful. At 20 features, NQAC achieves 90% accuracy vs 82% without error correction. The average of 10 results per configuration ensures statistical reliability.

Bayesian Optimization converges to optimal parameters near nesting_degree ≈ 12–13 and coupling_strength ≈ 0.25, achieving peak target accuracy of 0.84 in the parameter search. NQAC's gauge transformation averages out systematic biases, producing more precise and trustworthy feature subsets for downstream ML models.

Future Work
Extending NQAC to larger problems and alternative quantum codes

Planned directions include: partition function calculation using Mean Field Analysis / Monte Carlo Simulation to find free energy; testing alternative optimization techniques beyond Bayesian Optimization; implementing on larger-scale problems to stress-test NQAC scalability; exploring different embedding techniques on D-Wave; investigating alternative decoding algorithms beyond majority voting; and implementing different families of quantum error correcting codes.

Trading System Production

Andromeda: Systematic Options Screening Platform

Proprietary
Pricing Engine
Multi-Model
Vol Framework
0.10
Delta Threshold
Live
Real-Time Data
Problem
Manual options screening misses systematically mispriced contracts

Options markets contain thousands of contracts across strikes and expirations. Manually identifying mispriced opportunities is impractical. Traditional pricing models assume constant volatility, but real markets exhibit volatility smiles and term structure that create systematic mispricings detectable only with advanced volatility models.

Traders need a production tool that calibrates in real-time against market data, surfaces the highest-conviction opportunities, and tracks portfolio-level P&L with proper Greeks attribution.

Methodology
Proprietary volatility engine with ML-optimized calibration

The system calibrates a proprietary stochastic volatility model to live market data using evolutionary optimization. Model prices are computed via Fourier-based pricing methods with characteristic function inversion. Implied volatility is extracted from mid-prices using numerical root-finding.

Opportunities are scored using profit_score = IV x mispricing, filtered by a delta threshold of 0.10 to eliminate deep OTM noise. The system tracks positions via SQLite with full P&L, drawdown, and Greeks attribution.

Step 01

Market Data

Real-time options chains, 24h intraday price data, multi-ticker

Step 02

Vol Calibration

Evolutionary optimization fits model parameters to live market surface

Step 03

Model Pricing

Fourier-based method computes theoretical prices across strike grid

Step 04

Opportunity Scoring

Rank by IV x mispricing, filter by delta threshold 0.10

Step 05

Portfolio Tracking

SQLite P&L, drawdown curves, Greeks dashboard

Results
Production Streamlit application with full portfolio management

Andromeda is a fully operational trading system with five tabs: Portfolio (merged opportunities and positions), Greeks (live sensitivity dashboard), Scenarios (what-if P&L under vol/price shifts), Book (SQLite-backed position tracking with P&L and drawdown), and History (trade log). The dark-themed UI uses uniform styling with real-time 24h intraday charts and multi-ticker selection.

Published Research arXiv:2604.06068

Beyond Black-Scholes: A Computational Framework for Option Pricing Using Heston, GARCH, and Jump Diffusion Models

Authors: Karmanpartap Singh Sidhu, Pranshi Saxena
Affiliation: Viterbi School of Engineering, University of Southern California
Published: arXiv preprint, arXiv:2604.06068, April 2026
4
Models Implemented
5
Stocks Tested
100K
Monte Carlo Sims
L-BFGS-B
ML Optimizer
Situation
Black-Scholes assumptions fail in real markets

The Black-Scholes model assumes constant volatility, continuous price movements, no dividends, and European-style exercise only. In real markets, volatility fluctuates and clusters, asset prices exhibit sudden jumps (as seen during the 2008 financial crisis), and out-of-the-money put options are systematically mispriced. These limitations lead to inaccurate option valuations that expose traders and portfolio managers to unhedged risks.

Task
Develop a unified computational framework that addresses each limitation

Build and validate four progressively advanced option pricing models — each addressing a specific Black-Scholes shortcoming — using Monte Carlo simulation as the computational backbone. Introduce machine learning-based parameter optimization (L-BFGS-B) for the Heston and Merton models, and validate against live market prices across five stocks with diverse volatility profiles: TSLA, META, AMC, MARA, and SHOP.

Action
Four models with ML-optimized calibration

Each model builds on the previous, progressively removing Black-Scholes assumptions while using Monte Carlo simulation (10,000–100,000 paths) to generate stock price trajectories.

Model 01

Black-Scholes MC

GBM-based Monte Carlo with constant volatility. Baseline for comparison.

Model 02

Merton Jump Diffusion

Adds Poisson-distributed price jumps. ML-optimized λ, μⱼ, σⱼ via L-BFGS-B.

Model 03

GARCH(1,1)

Time-varying volatility from historical data. Forecasts future option prices 3 days out.

Model 04

Heston Stochastic Vol

Mean-reverting stochastic variance. ML-optimized κ, θ, σᵥ via L-BFGS-B.

The L-BFGS-B optimizer minimizes the loss function L(κ,θ,σᵥ) = Σ(σ²_obs,t − v_t)² by computing gradients and approximating the inverse Hessian matrix, enabling efficient parameter estimation with bound constraints to keep values realistic.

Results
Heston model consistently outperforms Black-Scholes across all stocks

META — Merton Jump Diffusion vs Market Price
Strike Merton Est. Market Error
$420$141.01$143.90-$2.89
$450$110.97$106.27+$4.70
$470$91.52$84.39+$7.13
$480$81.74$77.40+$4.34
$500$61.43$57.69+$3.74

Jump diffusion slightly overestimates at higher strikes due to incorporated jump risk. Underestimates at lowest strike ($420) where market premium is highest.

GARCH(1,1) — Predicted vs Actual Future Prices (AMC, 3-Day Forecast)
Strike Market (Nov 19) GARCH Est. Market (Nov 20) GARCH Est.
$3.00$1.18$1.42$1.34$1.45
$3.50$0.79$0.86$0.93$1.02
$4.00$0.35$0.32$0.42$0.46
$4.50$0.08$0.10$0.10$0.12
$5.00$0.04$0.08$0.05$0.07

GARCH predictions validated against actual market prices 1–2 days forward. Model captures directional movement correctly; slight overestimation at ITM strikes due to forecasted volatility exceeding realized.

Key findings: The Heston model consistently produced estimates closest to market across all stocks and strikes. GBM overestimates at low strikes and underestimates at high strikes due to constant volatility assumption. Merton jump diffusion excels for volatile assets (AMC, MARA) where sudden price movements dominate. GARCH provides reliable short-term forecasts when sufficient historical data is available for volatility clustering calibration.

Future Work
Neural networks and quantum walks for next-generation pricing

Planned extensions include neural network-based parameter optimization using LSTM networks for dynamic volatility forecasting, and exploration of quantum walks as a novel framework for simulating stock price and volatility routes — offering potential computational advantages and fresh insights into asset price movements.
